context:
This course examines how a variety of cultural traditions propose answers to the question of how to live a meaningful life. It considers the meaning of life, not as a philosophical abstraction, but as a question that individuals grapple with in their daily lives, facing difficult decisions between meeting and defying cultural expectations. The course also provides tools for thinking about moral decisions as social and historical practices, and permits students to compare and contextualize the ways people in different times and places approach fundamental ethical concerns.

Syllabus:
21A.157 The Meaning of Life

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Course Description:
This course examines how a variety of cultural traditions approach and propose answers to the question of how to live a meaningful life. We consider the meaning of life, not as a philosophical abstraction, but as a matter with which individuals grapple in their daily lives and communities, facing difficult decisions between meeting and defying cultural expectations. The course aims to provide tools for thinking about moral and ethical decisions as social and historical practices, and permits students to compare and contextualize how people in different times and places approach fundamental — though not necessarily universal — questions to do with such topics as family, wealth, self-cultivation, sex, belonging, and powers beyond.

Text:

Required:
Hamdy, Sherine, and Coleman Nye. Lissa: A Story about Medical Promise, Friendship, and Revolution. University of Toronto Press, 2017. ISBN: 9781487593476. [Preview with Google Books]

Suggested:
Kavedžija, Iza, and Harry Walker, eds. Values of Happiness: Toward an Anthropology of Purpose in Life. HAU, 2017. ISBN: 9780986132575.

All other readings can be found in the Readings section.

Grading:

| Activities                            | Percentage |
|---------------------------------------|------------|
| Attendance and participation          | 15%        |
| "Moral Moment" vignette               | 5%         |
| Investigation papers (3 × 20% each)   | 60%        |
| Final paper                           | 20%        |
exercise:
Course context:
This course examines how a variety of cultural traditions propose answers to the question of how to live a meaningful life. It considers the meaning of life, not as a philosophical abstraction, but as a question that individuals grapple with in their daily lives, facing difficult decisions between meeting and defying cultural expectations. The course also provides tools for thinking about moral decisions as social and historical practices, and permits students to compare and contextualize the ways people in different times and places approach fundamental ethical concerns.

Syllabus:
21A.157 The Meaning of Life

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Course Description:
This course examines how a variety of cultural traditions approach and propose answers to the question of how to live a meaningful life. We consider the meaning of life, not as a philosophical abstraction, but as a matter with which individuals grapple in their daily lives and communities, facing difficult decisions between meeting and defying cultural expectations. The course aims to provide tools for thinking about moral and ethical decisions as social and historical practices, and permits students to compare and contextualize how people in different times and places approach fundamental — though not necessarily universal — questions to do with such topics as family, wealth, self-cultivation, sex, belonging, and powers beyond.

Text:

Required:
Hamdy, Sherine, and Coleman Nye. Lissa: A Story about Medical Promise, Friendship, and Revolution. University of Toronto Press, 2017. ISBN: 9781487593476. [Preview with Google Books]

Suggested:
Kavedžija, Iza, and Harry Walker, eds. Values of Happiness: Toward an Anthropology of Purpose in Life. HAU, 2017. ISBN: 9780986132575.

All other readings can be found in the Readings section.

Grading:

| Activities                            | Percentage |
|---------------------------------------|------------|
| Attendance and participation          | 15%        |
| "Moral Moment" vignette               | 5%         |
| Investigation papers (3 × 20% each)   | 60%        |
| Final paper                           | 20%        |

Exercise:
TITLE: Final Paper

21A.157 The Meaning of Life
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Due: Last session
Weight: 20% of the final grade
Length: 1500–1600 words

Assignment:
Go back to the personal moral moment you wrote about in the first weeks of class. Drawing explicitly on (and citing) at least three readings from the semester, and using insights and concepts from the course (e.g., moral trial/laboratory, gender proficiency, achieved/ascribed status, agency (& negative agency), the 'I' and the 'Me', belonging, cultural citizenship, etc.), write an essay analyzing your own story through an anthropological lens.

One possible path is to contextualize the original moment socially, culturally, and/or historically. Another is to establish (by making an argumentative case for) parallels and/or points of contrast with other case studies that we have read. The general idea is to demonstrate what you've learned in the course by giving new meaning to your previous story.

Do not presume that your reader has your previous "moral moment" assignment at hand. Instead, summarize the key players and moments of the story that you're now contextualizing and interpreting.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hi, i need help w my final paper for 21A.157 rn. i have my moral moment, but idk how to turn it into an anthropological analysis.
exchanges.tutor: Sure, let's get started! To help you think about how to analyze your moral moment anthropologically, can you briefly describe what your moral moment was about? What happened, who was involved, and what made it feel like a moral decision?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is just starting, so I should orient them to the assignment without giving too much scaffolding. The best first step is to get them to articulate their moral moment, since everything in the final paper builds from that. I'll ask them to share it so we can work from there.

exchanges.student: mine was about choosing whether to tell my parents i wanted to switch majors. it felt moral bc it wasn’t just “my choice” — it affected family expectations, money, and like who i’m supposed to be.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong foundation — there's a lot going on there beyond just a personal choice. Now, thinking back to the course concepts listed in the assignment (things like ascribed/achieved status, the 'I' and the 'Me', belonging, moral trial/laboratory, etc.) — do any of those immediately jump out as relevant to your story? Which ones and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a rich moral moment with clear anthropological dimensions — family expectations, identity, economic considerations, and cultural scripts about who one is 'supposed to be.' Rather than pointing these out for them, I should ask a Socratic question that nudges them to identify the anthropological concepts from the course that might connect to their story. This moves the conversation toward the analytical work they need to do.

exchanges.student: i think belonging and maybe achieved vs ascribed status fit, but i’m kinda fuzzy on the difference tbh. does ascribed mean the role my family already put on me?
exchanges.tutor: You're close! Think about it this way: what's the difference between a status you're *born into* versus one you *work toward or choose*? How does that map onto your situation with your parents and your major?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is on the right track but needs to clarify their understanding of ascribed vs. achieved status before they can apply it. Rather than defining it for them, I should ask a question that helps them reason through the distinction themselves using their own story as the anchor.

exchanges.student: ok yeah, so ascribed is more like being the “good kid” or the one expected to do something practical, and achieved is the identity i’m trying to make for myself. i’m not 100% sure if “major” itself counts as status tho.
exchanges.tutor: Good, that's a solid grasp of the distinction. On your hesitation — think less about the major itself and more about what choosing a major *signals* to others and to yourself. Does that change how you see it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly grasped the ascribed/achieved distinction and applied it well to their story. Their hesitation about whether 'major' counts as status is worth pushing on — it shows good critical thinking. I should encourage them to think about what status is really about (social recognition, identity, expectations) rather than resolving it for them.

exchanges.student: yeah, that helps ngl. it’s not the major alone, it’s what it signals about success, responsibility, and whether i still belong in my family’s idea of a good future.
exchanges.tutor: That's a real insight — you're already thinking anthropologically. Now, which course readings come to mind when you think about those ideas of family expectations, belonging, and what a 'good future' looks like? The paper needs at least three.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a genuinely insightful connection — they've moved from the surface-level choice to the symbolic and social dimensions of it. This is exactly the kind of anthropological thinking the paper needs. Now is a good moment to push them toward the readings, since the assignment requires at least three cited sources. I should ask them which course readings connect to what they just articulated.

exchanges.student: Lissa def seems useful for family/social pressure stuff, but i’m blanking on the others rn. can u help me think of 2 kinds of readings i should look for in my notes?
exchanges.tutor: That's your job to find in your notes! But here's a nudge: your story touches on identity-making and family/cultural expectations. What course themes or topics — maybe from different weeks or units — dealt with those kinds of tensions? Start there and see what readings come up.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to do some of the intellectual work for them — identifying readings. I should not do that. Instead, I can prompt them to think about the course themes that relate to their story and trust them to make the connection to their notes. I'll give a nudge toward the thematic categories without naming readings for them.

exchanges.student: ok fair lol. maybe stuff on selfhood/the “I” and “Me,” and also readings on belonging or cultural citizenship? i just need a clearer hint on how to match those to my story.
exchanges.tutor: You've got the right themes — now do the connecting yourself. Pick one: the 'I' and the 'Me,' or belonging/cultural citizenship. How does that concept show up in what you experienced with your parents and the major decision? Take a stab at it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified two strong thematic areas on their own, which is good progress. They're now asking for a 'clearer hint' on how to connect them to their story — but this is the core analytical work of the paper, so I should not do it for them. Instead, I'll ask them to try making that connection themselves, starting with one of the two themes they identified.

exchanges.student: maybe the “Me” is the version of myself shaped by how my parents and community see me, and the “I” is the part pushing to choose differently. is that too simplistic?
exchanges.tutor: That's not too simplistic — it's actually a solid application. The question is: what does the *tension* between your 'I' and your 'Me' reveal about the moral weight of your decision? That tension is where your analysis lives.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly applied the I/Me distinction to their story in a way that's actually quite good. I should affirm that it's on the right track without overpraising, and push them to think about how this creates analytical depth rather than staying at the surface level. The goal is to move them toward integrating this into an argument, not just a description.

exchanges.student: i think it shows the choice felt moral bc i wasn’t just picking for myself — i was negotiating between self-direction and social obligation. idk how to turn that into a thesis tho.
exchanges.tutor: That insight is strong — hold onto it. Now try writing a rough thesis sentence in your own words. Don't worry about it being perfect. What is the central argument your paper is making about your moral moment?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has articulated a genuinely strong analytical insight that could form the core of a thesis. This is a pivotal moment — I should not write or suggest language for a thesis, but I can ask them to try formulating one themselves based on what they just said. I'll push them to attempt it in their own words.

exchanges.student: maybe: my decision to switch majors became a moral moment because it exposed a conflict between my own agency and my family’s expectations, showing how ideas of belonging and success shape what feels like a meaningful life.
exchanges.tutor: That's a good start — it captures the tension well. One thing to consider: your reader should be able to tell this is an *anthropological* analysis, not just a personal reflection. How could you signal more clearly that you're analyzing this through specific course concepts and readings?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a genuine thesis attempt that captures the core tension and connects it to course themes. It's quite good. However, I should give honest, constructive feedback without grading or writing for them. I can point out what's working and what could be sharper — specifically, the thesis could more explicitly signal the anthropological lens and the course concepts that will be used as analytical tools. I should ask them to think about that without rewriting it for them.

grade.overview: The tutor conducted an exemplary Socratic session throughout all 10 exchanges. No submission-ready content was provided, no student text was rewritten, and key ideas were consistently elicited from the student rather than given. The tutor scaffolded well, built on each student response, and maintained a supportive, concise, and appropriately formative tone throughout.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave the student a final answer, thesis, or submission-ready content. When the student asked for reading suggestions, the tutor explicitly redirected them to their own notes (Turn 6). The draft thesis in Turn 10 was entirely student-generated, and the tutor's response pushed for further refinement rather than polishing it. No deductions apply. 1.2: The tutor consistently built on student progress — acknowledging correct steps (e.g., 'That's a solid grasp,' 'That's a real insight') and advancing from there. Scaffolding was well-sequenced from moral moment description to concept application to thesis drafting. 1.3: The tutor provided process-level feedback, notably in Turn 10 pointing out that the thesis needed to signal anthropological analysis more clearly, which is specific methodological guidance. 2.1 and 2.2: No repetition without progress, no off-topic drift, and no misalignment with the assignment. 3.1 and 3.2: Responses were concise and interactive, tone was warm and encouraging without being evaluative or grade-implying.
